journal-title-group.journal-title: The Journal of Veterinary Medical Science

title-group.article-title: Relationship between the left–right asymmetric motor-related conformation and the orientation of facial hair whorls in Japanese Kiso horses

contrib-group.aff: 1)Laboratory of Veterinary Anatomy, Faculty of Applied Biological Sciences, Gifu University, Gifu, Japan
2)Laboratory of Veterinary Anatomy, The United Graduate School of Veterinary Science, Gifu University, Gifu, Japan

author-notes.corresp: *Correspondence to: Onouchi S: onouchi.sawa.b6@f.gifu-u.ac.jp, Laboratory of Veterinary Anatomy, Faculty of Applied Biological Sciences, Gifu University, 1-1 Yanagido, Gifu 501-1193, Japan

permissions.copyright-statement: ©2024 The Japanese Society of Veterinary Science

license.license-p: This is an open-access article distributed under the terms of the Creative Commons Attribution Non-Commercial No Derivatives (by-nc-nd) License. (CC-BY-NC-ND 4.0: https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc-nd/4.0/)

abstract.p: We examined the relationship between inherited motor-related conformation and orientation of facial hair whorls in Japanese Kiso horses. Eleven horses were divided into clockwise, counterclockwise, and radial groups according to facial hair whorls. We placed six markers on anatomical landmarks of each lateral side in a horse and measured the height of the landmarks, the distance between adjacent landmarks, and the angle of the adjacent landmarks. In the counterclockwise group, the horses tended to exhibit higher values on the left side than on the right side, and the comparison of the height of landmarks revealed a significant difference between both sides. Therefore, the orientation of facial hair whorls may suggest the tendency of motor-related conformation, at least in counterclockwise group.

body.p: The conformation of horses (Equus caballus) exhibits a left–right asymmetry. Coxal bones and femurs in thoroughbred horses exhibited a left–right asymmetry, which was considered the result of selection pressure for racing performance on curved racetracks and the result of asymmetric loading with hard training for races [2, 9, 14]. Therefore, a relationship exists between conformation and physical exercise. Horses tend to display motor laterality, e.g., a favorite turning direction and the use of one-sided forelimb [5,6,7]. Motor laterality was also considered the cause of asymmetric conformation. In contrast, horses possess an inherited asymmetry in the digital and pedal phalanges, which was related to the initiation of motor laterality, and these two factors influenced each other and intensified these phenomena [12, 13].

Horses possess the inherited asymmetric conformation not only in the motor system but also in hair whorls [6]. Facial hair whorls are used to identify individual horses, and there are traditional beliefs on the relationship between traits of facial hair whorls and characteristics of horses. The hair whorls have patterns in the direction of the flow of the hair from the center of a whorl, viz., clockwise, counterclockwise, or radial. It has been reported that a relationship exists between the orientation of facial hair whorls and the motor laterality [6].

Although a relationship might exist between the motor-related conformation, motor laterality, and orientation of facial hair whorls, these previous studies focused only on the relationship between the motor-related conformation and the motor laterality, or on the relationship between the orientation of facial hair whorls and the motor laterality, not on the relationship between the motor-related conformation and the orientation of facial hair whorls. Therefore, we explored the relationship between the motor-related conformation and the orientation of facial hair whorls in the present study.

We used Japanese Kiso horses [10, 11], primarily raised in the Kiso region located from southern Nagano Prefecture to eastern Gifu Prefecture, Japan, in the present study, because Kiso horses are not trained for races, but for horse riding for tourists, and are suitable for investigating the relationship between the inherited motor-related conformation and the orientation of facial hair whorls, which are also the inherited traits. We investigated the existence of left–right asymmetry in the motor-related conformation in each group of horses categorized according to the orientation of facial hair whorls. The present study could provide some estimates about characteristic body balance of horses before training or treating horses.

We examined 11 female Japanese Kiso horses (aged 1–20 years) born and raised in the Village of Kiso Horse located on Nagano Prefecture, Japan. We divided these horses into the following three groups according to the orientation of facial hair whorls: clockwise, counterclockwise, and radial (Fig. 2Fig. 2.
Grouping by the orientation of facial hair whorls. (A)–(C): Representative photos and schemas of clockwise, counterclockwise, and radial groups, respectively. (D): A case of three hair whorls categorized into the counterclockwise group. C, clockwise; CC, counterclockwise; R, radial.). The horses were handled according to the Guide for Care and Use of Animal Experimentation at Gifu University, Japan (Permission No. 2021-065).

In order to evaluate the conformation of a horse, we placed 12 markers (round shape of 5 cm in diameter, made of polypropylene) on the anatomical landmarks of the left and right sides of a horse body (Fig. 1Fig. 1.
Representative frame and schema for measurements. (A): A representative frame of the left side view. (B): A schema of the anatomical landmarks and the standard line. 1, tuber of scapula; 2, greater tubercle of humerus; 3, tuber of olecranon of ulna; 4, inner tip of coxal tuber of ilium; 5, outer tip of coxal tuber of ilium; 6, femoral greater trochanter.) and compared positioning of each marker between the left and right sides [12, 13]. These markers were located on each side of the tuber of the scapula, greater tubercle of humerus, tuber of olecranon of ulna, inner and outer tips of the coxal tuber of the ilium, and femoral greater trochanter. The same operator performed setting the markers in all examined horses. The walking of an examined horse with markers was recorded as a movie from the left side and then from the right side because fixing horses as a proper posture was difficult in Kiso horses which did not take such a training. During the recording, the camera (Tough TG-5, Olympus, Tokyo, Japan) was moved in the direction parallel to the walking. In the movie, the entire lateral body was contained within the frame, and the center of the frame focused on the middle abdominal region of the horse.

We used five frames of each movie from the left and right sides for each horse. Measured values were obtained as the average of values from the five frames each from the left and right sides. For the measurement, we selected a frame in which the horse completely grounded both the forelimb and hindlimb and the forelimb and hindlimb on the opposite side were closest to each other (Fig. 1A). The horizontal direction of each selected frame was corrected parallel to the standard line running between the front tips of the forehoof and hindhoof (Fig. 1B) using Adobe Photoshop Elements 2020 (Adobe, San Jose, CA, USA). In each corrected frame, three categories of measurement were performed in ImageJ (National Institute of Mental Health, Bethesda, MD, USA), as follows: the height of each marker on the anatomical landmarks from the standard line, the distance between two adjacent markers, and the angle between the line of two adjacent markers and the standard line, which are referred to as the height of landmarks, the distance between adjacent landmarks, and the angle of adjacent landmarks, respectively.

To avoid the influence of the distance and angle between the camera and the examined horse among frames from the two directions, the relative value was calculated for the height of landmarks and the distance between adjacent landmarks; the actual value of the height of landmarks as well as that of the distance between adjacent landmarks was divided by the height of the most highest point of the cluneal region (height of the hip) of the examined horse in each frame (Fig. 1B). The measured values of the height of landmarks and the distance between adjacent landmarks were rounded off to the second decimal place. The measured values of the angle of adjacent landmarks were rounded off to the closest whole number. All measurements were performed by the same operator.

In each group categorized according to the orientation of hair whorls, except for the clockwise group, the mean of individual values was compared between the left and right sides in each category of measurement, viz., the height of landmarks, the distance between adjacent landmarks, and the angle of adjacent landmarks. The mean of individual values was calculated as follows: for the height of landmarks, the individual values of six measured items were summed and divided by the number of measured items, six, on the left and right sides of each horse. For the distance between adjacent landmarks and the angle of adjacent landmarks, the individual values of five measured items were summed and divided by five on the left and right sides of each horse. The statistical difference in the mean of individual values between the left and right sides was examined using paired t-test (P<0.05). This statistical analysis was used to test the null hypothesis that no difference existed in the mean value between the left and right sides in each comparison. Before using paired t-test, we confirmed the normal distribution of the mean of individual values by quantile–quantile plot (Supplementary Fig. 1).

We divided 11 female Kiso horses into the following three groups: clockwise (n=1), counterclockwise (n=4), and radial (n=6) (Tables 1Table 1.Results of the height of landmarks

11R※117°113°17°16°7°9°40°33°23°17°41°38°
Avg. ± SD40°± 1°40°± 1°
※, Showing several facial hair whorls; Bold, higher than the other side; C, clockwise; CC, counterclockwise; R, radial., Fig. 2). Because of a few horses with a single hair whorl, horses with both counterclockwise and radial hair whorls (n=2) were considered the counterclockwise group (Fig. 2D). In addition, horses in radial group contained two horses with three radial hair whorls. The number of horses in each group was biased; the number in the radial group was the highest among the three groups, and only one horse was categorized into the clockwise group although we looked for horses with clockwise hair whorls in the Village. In thoroughbreds and mixed blood horses with thoroughbreds and Irish native breeds, both males and females showed the lowest population of the radial group (males: 15%, females: 4%) and almost similar population between the clockwise and counterclockwise groups [6]. This difference in the population of each group between the present study and the previous study might be due to differences in breeds and regions. Moreover, Kiso horses exhibited a high coefficient of inbreeding because the population of Kiso horses was restored by the backcrossing of one stallion, Daisan-haruyama, to avoid extinction after World War II [10, 11]. Therefore, the orientation of hair whorls might be affected by the characteristics of Daisan-haruyama, the ancestor of current Kiso horses.

The height of landmarks, the distance between adjacent landmarks, and the angle of adjacent landmarks measured on both lateral sides are shown in Tables 1, 2, 3. In the clockwise group (n=1), four of six values for the height of landmarks and four of five values for the angle of adjacent landmarks were higher on the right side than on the left side. Regarding the distance between adjacent landmarks, the number of higher values on the right side was similar to that on the left side (two of five values). In the counterclockwise group (n=4), the number of higher values on the left side exceeded that on the right side in two of three categories of measurements for all horses. In particular, the number of higher height values of landmarks on the left side was more than that on the right side for all horses, and the height of forelimb factors (the tuber of the scapula, greater tubercle of humerus, and tuber of olecranon of ulna) was higher on the left side for all horses (Table 1). In the radial group (n=6), the tendency of the side showing higher values depended on individual horses. We performed Student’s t-test to compare the mean of individual values in each category of measurements between the left and right sides in the counterclockwise and radial groups. In the comparison of the height of landmarks in the counterclockwise group, the null hypothesis was rejected (P=0.035), and the mean of individual values on the left side was significantly higher than that on the right side (Table 1). However, no significant differences were observed in each mean of individual values in the radial group.

Therefore, the present study suggested that the horse in the counterclockwise group had a longer left forelimb than a right forelimb. For the motor system in horses, the forelimbs play a vital role in holding body weight and steering the direction of movement, and the hindlimbs provide the driving force [1, 8]. Therefore, the forelimbs and hindlimbs are affected by the loading resulting from the balance of body and hard training, respectively. Hence, Kiso horses without any hard training for races exhibited the asymmetric conformation, particular in the forelimbs, related to inherited factors such as an unbalanced body, and the orientation of facial hair whorls suggested the tendency of features regarding the motor-related conformation, at least in counterclockwise group. From the viewpoint of motor laterality, the higher values of the height of forelimb factors on the left side might indicate that a horse in the counterclockwise group had a longer left forelimb than a right forelimb, indicating that the horse has an advantage of turning to the right side and that the short right forelimb was the result of a larger loading on the right side due to frequent use than on the left side [3, 4]. In other words, horses in the counterclockwise group might exhibit right-sidedness in the present study. In a previous study that explored the relationship between the motor laterality and the orientation of facial hair whorls [6], horses in the counterclockwise group tended to exhibit left-sidedness. This difference in the orientation of the motor laterality between the present study and the previous study might be due to differences in breeds, and/or this difference might be because the present study only assumed, and not evaluated, the actual motor laterality. Therefore, further investigation is required in this regard.

Finally, we demonstrated that the orientation of facial hair whorls could be related to the asymmetry of the inherited motor-related conformation, particularly in the height of landmarks, in Kiso horses. Despite the small number of examined horses, we were able to obtain this interesting result partially because Kiso horses exhibited a high coefficient of inbreeding and low individual variation. On the other hand, due to the small number, we need to continue more investigation and to elucidate the relationship between the motor-related conformation, orientation of facial hair whorls, and motor laterality, including age-dependence, sexual-dependence and so on in Kiso horses.

sec.title: CONFLICT OF INTEREST
sec.p: The authors declare no conflicts of interest associated with this manuscript.

ack.p: We would like to thank Mr. T. Nakagawa in the Village of Kiso Horse for his permission for our study and giving information about horses, as well as students in our laboratory for their help to taking movies.
